narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've poured into your path toward law school, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your bar exam journey tipping toward a 49.8% chance of not passing, leading to this tough call on your application. Picture the model piecing together your story like chapters in a book: your first-year grades landed you in the bottom decile at school—that was the strongest signal, whispering early struggles that often echo through law studies. Then came your third-year decile, also at 1.0, reinforcing that pattern of being at the lower end amid your peers, making it the second-biggest pull toward expecting hurdles ahead. Your middle family income added another layer, as it sometimes means fewer resources to buffer the intense demands of bar prep compared to higher brackets. Your Native American background factored in next, carrying historical weight in outcomes that the model couldn't ignore. Even your LSAT score of 44.0 and undergrad GPA of 3.1, while showing real grit, nudged the outlook downward in this mix. Being female and planning full-time studies rounded out the picture, each tilting slightly toward the challenges we've seen in similar profiles. It's not that any one piece defined you—it's how they wove together into a narrative of predicted risk that felt too high for us to endorse right now. This isn't a judgment on your potential or heart; it's the model's honest read based on patterns from thousands like yours. Many students in your shoes have turned things around by targeting those early grade dips—perhaps with focused tutoring or study overhauls. You're capable of rewriting this story, and I truly believe in the strength you bring as a full-time trailblazer from your background. Keep pushing; law needs voices like yours.
